items.q: Why are triangles used in structures instead of rectangles?
items.correct: A triangle cannot change shape without changing the length of a side, so it is inherently rigid; a rectangle can rack sideways into a parallelogram unless braced.
items.misconception: Triangles are stronger because they contain more material than other shapes.
items.random_wrong: Triangles are used because they are the cheapest shape to paint.
items.why: Students attribute strength to material quantity; the insight is geometric rigidity, which is why one diagonal brace stabilises a whole rectangular frame.

items.q: Where do tension and compression occur in a loaded beam?
items.correct: In a simply supported beam bending under a central load, the top surface is in compression and the bottom surface is in tension, with a neutral axis between.
items.misconception: The whole beam is in tension when a load is applied.
items.random_wrong: Tension only occurs at the two supports of the beam.
items.why: Students assign one force to the whole member; seeing bending as compression and tension on opposite faces explains why beams fail from the bottom in tension.

items.q: What is the difference between a strut and a tie?
items.correct: A strut carries compression and must resist buckling; a tie carries tension and can even be a cable, since pulling forces keep it taut.
items.misconception: Strut and tie are interchangeable names for any structural member.
items.random_wrong: A strut is the horizontal floor panel of a structure.
items.why: Naming members by the force they carry is the foundation of structural analysis; mixing them up makes bridge and roof truss questions impossible.

items.q: Why does triangulation make a frame rigid?
items.correct: A triangle cannot change shape without changing a side length
items.misconception: Triangles contain more material than squares
items.random_wrong: Triangles weigh less than any other shape
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A triangle cannot change shape without changing a side length. “Triangles contain more material than squares” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which structural member is designed to resist tension?
items.why: It's A tie. “A strut” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a simply supported beam loaded at its centre, where is the compression?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Along the top surface. “Along the bottom surface” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which change makes a free-standing structure more stable?
items.correct: Lowering its centre of gravity and widening its base
items.why: Correct answer: Lowering its centre of gravity and widening its base. “Raising its centre of gravity” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A 600 N load is shared equally by the three legs of a stool. What force does each leg carry?
items.why: Exact answer: 200 N. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
